title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Forward Problems
We are often trying to work with a model that turns input x∈ℝnx \in \mathbb{R}^{n} into output y∈ℝmy\in \mathbb{R}^{m}:
y←ϕθ(x), y \gets \phi_{\theta}(x),
where θ∈ℝp\theta \in \mathbb{R}^p are the parameters of the model ϕθ\phi_{\theta}.

Example: Given joint angles qq, where is the robot arm’s ‘hand’? (Forward Kinematics)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Inverse Problems
The inverse problem is to find xx given yy and parameters θ\theta
x← solution of y=ϕθ(x), x \gets \text{ solution of } y = \phi_{\theta}(x),
where θ∈ℝp\theta \in \mathbb{R}^p are the parameters of the model ϕθ\phi_{\theta}.

Example: Given where we want the robot’s arm to be, what values should we set the joint angles qq to? (Inverse Kinematics)

We can achieve that if dL(x)dt=−eT(x)Be(x)\frac{ \mathrm{ d} L(x)}{\mathrm{ d} t} =  - e^T(x) B e(x)
⟹\implies solve J(x)ẋ=Be(x)J(x) \dot x = B e(x) for ẋ\dot x where B=BT≻0B = B^T \succ 0

Steepest descent: ẋ(t)=JT(x)e(x)\dot x(t) =   J^T(x) e(x)
Gauss-Newton direction: ẋ(t)=J+e(x)\dot x(t) = J^{+} e(x)1

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Solutions



Global minimizer


A point x*x^{*} is a global minimizer if f(x*)≤f(x)f(x^*) \leq f(x)1 for all x∈ℝnx \in \mathbb R^n.






Local minimizer


A point x*x^{*} is a local minimizer if there is a neighborhood 𝒩\mathcal N of x*x^* where f(x*)≤f(x)f(x^*) \leq f(x) for all x∈𝒩x \in \mathcal N, where a neighborhood of yy is an open set containing yy.






Strict local minimizer


A point x*x^{*} is a strict local minimizer if there is a neighborhood 𝒩\mathcal N of x*x^* where f(x*)<f(x)f(x^*) < f(x) for all x∈𝒩x \in \mathcal N with x≠x*x \neq x^*.



f(x)f(x) in optimizer is L(θ)L(\theta) in curve fitting

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Overview of Algorithms

Recall that the algorithms are iterative
We need a starting point 𝐱0\mathbf x_0
… and a method to produce iterates 𝐱1\mathbf{x}_1, 𝐱2\mathbf{x}_2,…\dots
Generally two methods:

Line Search
Trust Region








Note


The words method and algorithm are used interchangeably

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Line Search Methods

The algorithm chooses a direction pk∈ℝnp_k \in \mathbb{R}^n
Then, minimize f(x)f(x) on the line defined by 𝐱k+αpk\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha p_k. That is, solve minα>0f(𝐱k+αpk)(1)\min_{\alpha>0}\quad f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha p_k) \qquad(1)

In practice choose an α\alpha that makes f(𝐱k+1)<f(𝐱k)f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) < f(\mathbf{x}_{k}), not solve Equation 1.
Practical method uses back-tracking line search to pick α\alpha given pkp_k

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Line Search Methods

Two common choices for directions.

Negative gradient (steepest descent) pk=−∇f(𝐱k)p_k = - \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k})
Newton direction: pk=−(∇2f(𝐱k))−1∇f(𝐱k)p_k = - (\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_{k}))^{-1}\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k})

Lipschitz Function
A function f:ℝm→ℝf \colon \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R} is Lipschitz on a set SS if for any x,y∈Sx,y \in S ∥f(x)−f(y)∥≤L∥x−y∥ \|f(x) - f(y) \| \leq L \|x - y\|
For twice differentiable functions, ∇f\nabla f being differentiable is equivalent to ∇2f(w)≼LI \nabla^2 f(w) \preceq L I for all w∈Sw \in S

Descent Lemma


Let ff be a twice differentiable function whose gradient ∇f\nabla f is Lipschitz continuous over some convex set SS. Then, for all x,y∈Sx,y \in S, by Taylor’s Theorem, f(y)=f(x)+∇f(x)T(y−x)+12(y−x)T∇2f(z)(y−x) f(y) = f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y-x) + \frac{1}{2} (y-x)^T \nabla^2 f(z) (y-x)
⟹f(y)≤f(x)+∇f(x)T(y−x)+L2∥y−x∥2, \implies f(y) \leq f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y-x) + \frac{L}{2} \|y-x\|^2,



This Lemma says that we can define a local quadratic function that is an upper bound for ff near a point xx.

If x=xkx = x_k and y=xk−αk∇fky = x_k - \alpha_k \nabla f_k, we get fk+1≤fk−αk(2−Lαk)2∥∇fk∥2 f_{k+1} \leq f_k -\frac{\alpha_{k} (2 - L \alpha_{k} )}{2} \|\nabla f_k\|^2



If 0<αk<2L0 < \alpha_k < \frac{2}{L}, then fk+1<fkf_{k+1} < f_{k}.
fk−fk+1f_{k}-f_{k+1} approaches zero only when ∇fk→0\nabla f_k \to 0

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Motivation

We know that whatever point we are looking must be a stationary one, it must satisfy ∇f(x)=0\nabla f(x) = 0.
Newton’s method applies the Newton root-finding algorithm to ∇f\nabla f:

To solve g(x)=0g(x) = 0, iteratively solve 1 g(xk)+(xk+1−xk)T∇g(x)=0⟹xk+1=xk−(∇g(x))♯g(x)g(x_k) + (x_{k+1} - x_k)^T \nabla g(x) = 0 \implies x_{k+1} = x_k - (\nabla g(x) )^{\sharp} g(x)

Apply root finding to ∇f(x)=0\nabla f(x) = 0: xk+1=xk−(∇2f(x))−1∇f(x)x_{k+1} = x_k - (\nabla^2 f(x) )^{-1} \nabla f(x)

♯\sharp is the left pseudo-inverse: A♯=(ATA)−1ATA^\sharp = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T

text:
Alternate Motivation
When ∇2fk≻0\nabla^2 f_k \succ 0, we are minimizing the quadratic approximation f(x)≈fk+∇fkT(x−xk)+12(x−xk)T∇2fk(x−xk),f(x) \approx f_k + \nabla f_k^T (x-x_k) + \frac{1}{2} (x-x_k)^T \nabla^2 f_k (x-x_k), which need not have minimum at x⋆x^\star




Example: Quadratic Function


Consider f(x)=xTQx−bTx for Q=QT≻0,f(x) = x^T Q x - b^T x \quad \text{ for }Q=Q^T \succ 0, where x⋆=Q−1bx^\star = Q^{-1} b  Ideal update: xk+1=xk−(xk−x⋆)=xk−1⋅(xk−Q−1b)Newton update:xk+1=xk+1⋅(−1)⋅Q−1(Qxk−b)=xk−1⋅(xk−Q−1b)Steepest descent:xk+1=xk+αk⋅(−1)⋅(Qxk−b)\begin{align}
\text{ Ideal update: }&& x_{k+1} &= x_k - (x_k - x^\star) = x_k - 1 \cdot (x_k - Q^{-1} b )\\
\text{Newton update:}&& x_{k+1} &= x_k + 1 \cdot (-1) \cdot Q^{-1} (Q x_k - b) = x_k -1 \cdot (x_k - Q^{-1} b)\\
\text{Steepest descent:}&& x_{k+1} &= x_k + \alpha_k \cdot (-1) \cdot (Q x_k - b)
\end{align}

Convergence Rate






Theorem 3.5


Suppose that ff is twice differentiable and that the Hessian ∇2f\nabla^2 f is Lipschitz continuous in a neighborhood of a solution x⋆x^\star at which the sufficient conditions are satisfied. Consider the iteration xk+1=xk+pkx_{k+1} = x_k + p_k where pk=−∇2fk−1∇fkp_k = - \nabla^2 f_k^{-1} \nabla f_k. Then

If x0x_0 is sufficiently close to x⋆x^\star, then xk→x⋆x_k \to x^\star
The rate of convergence {xk}\{x_k\} is quadratic
The sequence of gradient norms {∥∇fk∥}\{\|\nabla f_k\|\} converges quadratically to zero

text: Newton’s Revenge

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Modifications



Although Newton’s method is very attractive in terms of its convergence properties the solution, it requires modification before it can be used at points that are far from the solution.



The following modifications are typically used:

Step-size reduction (damping)
Modifying Hessian to be positive definite
Approximation of Hessian

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Damping
A search parameter α\alpha is introduced 𝐱k+1=𝐱k−αk∇2f(𝐱k)−1∇f(𝐱k), \bm{x}_{k+1} = \bm{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla^2 f(\bm{x}_k)^{-1}\nabla
f(\bm{x}_k),  where αk\alpha_k is selected to minimize ff.
A popular selection method is backtracking line search.

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Positive Definiteness and Scaling
General class of algorithms is given by 𝐱k+1=𝐱k+αpk=𝐱k−αBk∇fk,(2) \bm{x}_{k+1} = \bm{x}_k + \alpha p_k = \bm{x}_k - \alpha B_k \nabla f_k,  \qquad(2)

SD: Bk=𝐈B_k = \bm{I}, Newton: Bk=∇2f(𝐱k)−1B_k = \nabla^2 f(\bm{x}_k)^{-1}.

For small α\alpha, it can be shown that f(𝐱k+1)=f(𝐱k)−α∇fkTBk∇fk+O(α2). f(\bm{x}_{k+1}) = f(\bm{x}_k) -
\alpha \nabla f_k^T B_k \nabla f_k + O(\alpha^2). 

As α→0\alpha \rightarrow 0, the second term on the rhs dominates the third.
To guarantee a decrease in ff, we must have ∇fkTBk∇fk>0\nabla f_k^T B_k \nabla f_k
> 0.

Simplest way to ensure this is to require Bk≻𝟎B_k \succ \bm{0}.

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Positive Definiteness and Scaling

In practice, Newton’s method must be modified to accommodate the possible non-positive definiteness of ∇2f\nabla^2 f at regions far from the solution.
Common approach: Bk=[μk𝐈+∇2f(𝐱k)]−1B_k = [\mu_k\bm{I} +
\nabla^2 f(\bm{x}_k)]^{-1} for some μk>0\mu_k > 0.
This can be regarded as a compromise between SD (μk\mu_k very large) and Newton’s method (μk=0\mu_k = 0).

Levenberg-Marquardt performs Cholesky factorization for a given value of μk\mu_k as follows μk𝐈+∇2f(𝐱k)=𝐆T𝐆.\mu_k \bm{I} + \nabla^2 f(\bm{x}_k) = \bm{G}^T\bm{G}. 

This factorization checks implicitly for positive definiteness.
If the factorization fails (matrix not PD) μk\mu_k is increased.
Step direction is found by solving 𝐆T𝐆pk=−∇fk\bm{G}^T\bm{G} p_k = -\nabla f_k.

You can now minimize f(x)f(x) (when unconstrained) iteratively. Find LM x⋆x^\star by

Steepest descent with constant step size is easy and reliable but slow
Newton method is fast but expensive and unreliable

needs modifications for reliable convergence

Quasi-newton methods are a balance, but tricky

Choosing step size αk\alpha_k

Back-tracking for SD and modified NM improves performance
Strong Wolfe conditions for Quasi-newton methods

Not tackled: constraints, expensive ff/∇f\nabla f, discrete xx, non-smooth ff

Course Overview






Learning Goal


For you to be able to read and assimilate both recent papers and early foundational papers on robotics. Furthermore, develop programming skills that enable you to implement and try (possibly new) methods in simulation.

title: Overview
text:
Course Overview
Learning Goals:

Understand coordinates and robot configurations.
Understand how to transform the robot configuration into task coordinates and vice versa, along with the challenges in these processes.
Learn approaches to planning motions in both task coordinates and robot configurations.
Learn approaches to achieving planned motions using feedback control.
Understand the challenges of state estimation.
Learn optimization-based approaches to planning and control.
Understand contact modeling and control challenges.
Simulate robotic systems and test control algorithms.

title: MuJoCo and XML
text:
Actuated Example

import mujoco
import mujoco.viewer
import time 

# Load a model from a file (won't do this here to avoid external file calls)
model = mujoco.MjModel.from_xml_path('../models/simple_pendulum_act.xml')


dt: float = 0.002
model.opt.timestep = dt
data = mujoco.MjData(model)
data.qpos[0]=0.01

# Create a viewer
viewer = mujoco.viewer.launch_passive(model, data,
        show_left_ui=False,
        show_right_ui=False,
      )
try:
    while viewer.is_running():
        step_start = time.time()
        # Step the simulation
        data.ctrl[0] = -100*data.qpos[0] - 20*data.qvel[0]
        print(data.qpos[0])
        mujoco.mj_step(model, data)
        # Update the viewer
        viewer.sync()
        # Add a small delay to control frame rate
        time_until_next_step = dt - (time.time() - step_start)
        if time_until_next_step > 0:
            time.sleep(time_until_next_step)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("Viewer closed by user")
finally:
    viewer.close()

title: Python Setup
text:
Packages
We use the following major packages in python:

numpy: working with \(n\)-D arrays
mujoco : Modeling and simulation of robotic systems
matplotlib: plotting

The following command uses pip to install these python packages
pip install numpy mujoco matplotlib
If you want to use machine learning, consider the following packages:

torch: training of differentiable models
torchvision: tools for vision models
pandas: working with data
tqdm: progress bars in the command line
scikit-learn: source some classic datasets


Virtual Environment
Virtual environments allow you to install a custom set of packages with custom package versions for a project.
Inside the robotics-course directory, run
python -m venv code-venv
to create a blank virtual environment.
Activate it, and then install packages. Upon activation, the command line prompt changes to start with the name of the virtual environment. Here, it would be (code-venv):
source code-venv/bin/activate
pip install <packages above>
When you are done, you can run deactivate to return to the default global environment.

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
What is ICP?
The Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm is a fundamental technique for aligning 2D point clouds, particularly in LiDAR-based localization and mapping.

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Why 2D LiDAR Scan Matching?

Robot Navigation: Aligning sensor data with map data
SLAM: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
Autonomous Driving: Precise vehicle positioning
Industrial Robotics: Object positioning and manipulation

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
The Challenge
Given two 2D point clouds: - Source cloud: Current LiDAR scan - Target cloud: Reference map or previous scan
Goal: Find the optimal transformation (rotation + translation) to align source to target.

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Mathematical Formulation
We want to minimize the distance between corresponding points:
\[
\min_{T} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \| T(p_i) - q_i \|^2
\]
Where: - \(p_i\) = points in source cloud - \(q_i\) = corresponding points in target cloud - \(T\) = transformation matrix

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Step-by-Step Process

Initialization: Start with an initial guess of transformation
Correspondence: Find closest points between source and target
Transformation: Compute optimal transformation from correspondences
Iteration: Apply transformation and repeat until convergence

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Key Components

Point-to-point distance: Direct distance between corresponding points
Point-to-plane distance: Distance to tangent plane (more robust)
Error metric: Sum of squared distances
Optimization: Least squares solution for transformation

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Algorithm Steps

Initialize: Set \(T^{(0)} = I\) (identity transformation)
Iterate until convergence:

Find correspondences: For each point in source, find closest point in target
Compute transformation: Solve for optimal \(T^{(k+1)}\)
Update: \(T^{(k+1)} = T^{(k)} \cdot T^{(k+1)}\)
Check convergence: If \(\|T^{(k+1)} - T^{(k)}\| < \epsilon\), stop

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Finding Correspondences
# Pseudocode for correspondence finding
for each point p_i in source:
    find q_i = argmin_{q_j in target} ||p_i - q_j||^2

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Transformation Computation
For point-to-point ICP:
\[
T = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta & -\sin\theta & t_x \\
\sin\theta & \cos\theta & t_y \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\]
Where: - \(\theta\) = rotation angle - \(t_x, t_y\) = translation components

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Practical Challenges

Initial alignment: Poor initial guess leads to local minima
Noise: Sensor noise affects correspondence finding
Outliers: Misaligned points can corrupt the solution
Computational complexity: Large point clouds require optimization

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Optimization Techniques

KD-tree: Efficient nearest neighbor search
Sampling: Use subset of points for computation
Robust statistics: Handle outliers with RANSAC
Multi-resolution: Start with coarse alignment

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Typical Results
graph LR
    A[Source Scan] --> B{ICP}
    B --> C[Aligned Scan]
    C --> D[Target Map]

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Convergence Behavior

Fast initial convergence: Large alignment errors
Slow final convergence: Fine alignment adjustments
Stable solution: When transformation changes little between iterations

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Robotics

SLAM systems: Real-time localization
Autonomous navigation: Path planning and obstacle avoidance
Warehouse automation: Precise robot positioning

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Mapping

Mobile mapping: Building accurate 2D maps
Lidar mapping: High-precision geospatial data
Urban navigation: GPS-denied environments

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Common Issues

Local minima: Algorithm may get stuck in suboptimal alignment
Convergence speed: Can be slow for large point clouds
Noise sensitivity: Performance degrades with high noise
Featureless environments: Lack of distinctive points

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Solutions

Multiple initializations: Try different starting poses
Hierarchical approach: Multi-scale alignment
Feature extraction: Use distinctive points for matching
Sensor fusion: Combine with other sensors (IMU, odometry)

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Summary
The ICP algorithm provides an effective method for 2D LiDAR scan matching through iterative optimization. It’s widely used in robotics and mapping applications due to its simplicity and effectiveness.

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Key Takeaways

ICP is a fundamental technique for point cloud registration
Requires good initial alignment for optimal results
Can be extended with robust statistics and multi-resolution approaches
Essential for modern autonomous systems requiring precise localization

title: ICP Algorithm for 2D LiDAR Scan Matching
text:
Future Directions

Deep learning integration: Neural networks for correspondence finding
Real-time optimization: Hardware acceleration for faster processing
3D extensions: Generalization to 3D point clouds
Sensor fusion: Integration with other sensor modalities

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Instructions

Teams of up to two individuals
Input: 2D laser scans in sims_N_scans.npz or .mat
Output: Sequence of poses (Xi,Yi,θi)(X_i, Y_i, \theta_i)

2D plot of path (Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i)
Lines indicating orientation θi\theta_i

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
2D LiDAR Sensor



Rotating mirror/sensor emits laser pulses
Scans across a horizontal or vertical plane
Returns points in polar coordinates (ri,ϕi)(r_i, \phi_i)
Corresponds to nearest reflective object along direction ϕi\phi_i





A relatively inexpensive 2D LiDAR

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
LiDAR Data Format
Points returned in polar coordinates (ri,ϕi)(r_i, \phi_i)
Converted to Cartesian coordinates: xi=ricosϕiyi=risinϕi
\begin{aligned}
x_i &= r_i \cos \phi_i \\
y_i &= r_i \sin \phi_i
\end{aligned}







Note


Data provided in Cartesian coordinates with units in meters

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Scan Matching: Core Idea
When a robot moves, the LiDAR captures distances from different positions

Key insight: If motion is small, we can estimate it by aligning consecutive scans



Points corresponding to the same physical object should overlap
“Walls” in nearby scans should lie on top of each other

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Scan Matching: Visualization


Before alignment
Two scans in their respective robot frames

After alignment
Correctly transformed scans overlap


Scan matching - unaligned (left) vs aligned (right)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Robot Pose
The pose of the robot in a world frame is described by three numbers:
(Xi,Yi,θi)(X_i, Y_i, \theta_i)

(Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i): Position of robot (origin of robot frame) in world frame
θi\theta_i: Orientation angle (direction where ϕ=0\phi = 0 points)


When robot frame coincides with world frame: pose is (0,0,0)(0, 0, 0)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Coordinate Transformation
Given scan points {(xj,yj)}\left\{(x_j, y_j)\right\} in the robot frame at pose (Xi,Yi,θi)(X_i, Y_i, \theta_i) in the current frame FF:



xF=Xi+xjcosθi−yjsinθiyF=Yi+xjsinθi+yjcosθi
\begin{aligned}
x_{F} &= X_i + x_j \cos \theta_i - y_j \sin \theta_i \\
y_{F} &= Y_i + x_j \sin \theta_i + y_j \cos \theta_i
\end{aligned}





This transformation transforms all points in from the robot frame to the frame FF

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Scan Matching as Optimization
To match two scans:

Treat one scan as the reference frame
Find (Xi,Yi,θi)(X_i, Y_i, \theta_i) that transforms the second scan to align with the reference




Goal: Find the transformation that provides the best alignment

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Project Goal
Estimate the sequence of poses {(Xi,Yi,θi)}\{(X_i, Y_i, \theta_i)\} from a sequence of scans

Three components needed:

Evaluation function: Scalar measure of alignment quality
Optimization algorithm: Minimize the evaluation function
Path integration: Convert relative transformations to global trajectory

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Evaluation Function
How “good” is a candidate transformation (X,Y,θ)(X, Y, \theta)?

Common approach: Sum of squared distances between transformed points and reference L(X,Y,θ)=∑jdj2
L(X, Y, \theta) = \sum_j d_j^2



Challenge: The same physical point is mostly not observed in both scans

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Optimization
Given two scans and an evaluation function:

A rotation by angle θ\theta in 2D is represented by:

Homogeneous Coordinates
To compose transformations easily, use homogeneous coordinates:
[x′y′1]=[cosθ−sinθtxsinθcosθty001][xy1]
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & t_x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}


This 3×33 \times 3 matrix is an element of SE(2) — the Special Euclidean group in 2D

section: SE(2): The Group of 2D Rigid Motions
text:
SE(2): The Group of 2D Rigid Motions
T=[R𝐭𝟎1]∈SE(2)
T = \begin{bmatrix} R & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in SE(2)


Why a group?

Closure: T1T2∈SE(2)T_1 T_2 \in SE(2) (composing motions gives a motion)
Identity: I3×3I_{3\times 3} (no motion)
Inverse: T−1=[RT−RT𝐭𝟎1]T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T\mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0} & 1 \end{bmatrix} (undo motion)

def make_se2(theta, tx, ty):
    """Create SE(2) transformation matrix."""
    c, s = np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)
    return np.array([[c, -s, tx], [s, c, ty], [0, 0, 1]])

def transform_points(T, points):
    """Apply SE(2) transformation to 2D points."""
    ones = np.ones((1, points.shape[1]))
    homog = np.vstack([points, ones])
    result = T @ homog
    return result[:2]

text:
Point Cloud Alignment Problem
Given: Two point sets 𝒫={pi}\mathcal{P} = \{p_i\} and 𝒬={qi}\mathcal{Q} = \{q_i\}
Find: Transformation (R,𝐭)(R, \mathbf{t}) such that Rpi+𝐭≈qiR p_i + \mathbf{t} \approx q_i

As optimization: minR,𝐭∑i∥Rpi+𝐭−qi∥2
\min_{R, \mathbf{t}} \sum_{i} \| R p_i + \mathbf{t} - q_i \|^2



Connection to least squares: Each term is a residual! ri(R,𝐭)=Rpi+𝐭−qi
r_i(R, \mathbf{t}) = R p_i + \mathbf{t} - q_i

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
The Correspondence Problem
Key challenge: We usually don’t know which pip_i matches which qjq_j!


Same physical point may not be observed in both scans
Points have different indices in each scan
Some points have no match at all



Solution: Iteratively estimate correspondences and transformation

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Iterative Closest Point (ICP)
Core idea: Alternate between:

Finding correspondences: For each point, find its nearest neighbor
Solving for transformation: Given correspondences, find best (R,𝐭)(R, \mathbf{t})


Repeat until convergence

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
ICP Algorithm: Pseudocode
Input: Source points P, Target points Q, initial guess T₀
Output: Transformation T aligning P to Q

T ← T₀
repeat:
    P' ← apply T to P

    # Step 1: Find correspondences
    for each p' in P':
        c(p') ← nearest neighbor of p' in Q

    # Step 2: Compute optimal transformation
    T_new ← solve_alignment(P', correspondences)
    T ← T_new ∘ T

until convergence
return T

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
ICP Step 1: Nearest Neighbor
For each transformed source point p′ip'_i, find: c(i)=argminj∥p′i−qj∥
c(i) = \arg\min_j \| p'_i - q_j \|


Implementation:

Brute force: O(nm)O(nm) for nn source, mm target points
KD-tree: O(nlogm)O(n \log m) average case

text:
ICP Implementation

import numpy as np

def nearest_neighbors(source, target):
    """Find nearest neighbor in target for each source point."""
    indices = []
    for p in source.T:
        dists = np.linalg.norm(target.T - p, axis=1)
        indices.append(np.argmin(dists))
    return np.array(indices)

def compute_transformation(source, target_matched):
    """Compute optimal R, t using SVD."""
    # Centroids
    centroid_s = source.mean(axis=1, keepdims=True)
    centroid_t = target_matched.mean(axis=1, keepdims=True)
    # Center points
    s_centered = source - centroid_s
    t_centered = target_matched - centroid_t
    # SVD
    H = s_centered @ t_centered.T
    U, _, Vt = np.linalg.svd(H)
    R = Vt.T @ U.T
    # Ensure proper rotation (det = 1)
    if np.linalg.det(R) < 0:
        Vt[-1, :] *= -1
        R = Vt.T @ U.T
    t = centroid_t - R @ centroid_s
    return R, t.flatten()

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
ICP: Full Algorithm

def icp(source, target, max_iters=50, tol=1e-6):
    """
    Iterative Closest Point algorithm.
    source, target: 2×N arrays of 2D points
    Returns: R (2×2), t (2,), history of errors
    """
    R_total = np.eye(2)
    t_total = np.zeros(2)
    errors = []

    current = source.copy()
    for _ in range(max_iters):
        # Step 1: Find correspondences
        indices = nearest_neighbors(current, target)
        matched = target[:, indices]

        # Compute error
        error = np.mean(np.linalg.norm(current - matched, axis=0))
        errors.append(error)

        # Step 2: Compute transformation
        R, t = compute_transformation(current, matched)

        # Apply transformation
        current = R @ current + t.reshape(2, 1)

        # Accumulate
        R_total = R @ R_total
        t_total = R @ t_total + t

        # Check convergence
        if len(errors) > 1 and abs(errors[-2] - errors[-1]) < tol:
            break

    return R_total, t_total, errors

Numerical Gradient Check
Verify your Jacobian by comparing to finite differences:

def numerical_gradient(f, x, eps=1e-5):
    """Compute gradient numerically using central differences."""
    grad = np.zeros_like(x)
    for i in range(len(x)):
        x_plus = x.copy(); x_plus[i] += eps
        x_minus = x.copy(); x_minus[i] -= eps
        grad[i] = (f(x_plus) - f(x_minus)) / (2 * eps)
    return grad

# Example: cost function for alignment
def alignment_cost(params, source, target_matched):
    theta, tx, ty = params
    R = rotation_matrix(theta)
    transformed = R @ source + np.array([[tx], [ty]])
    return np.sum((transformed - target_matched)**2)

# Test

Lesson Overview

Motivation: Why an alternative to ICP?
NDT Representation: Discretizing space into Gaussian cells
Alignment Algorithm: Optimization over continuous transformation
Comparison: NDT vs ICP trade-offs


Key insight: Represent the target as a distribution, not discrete points

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Limitations of ICP
Recall ICP alternates between:

Finding nearest neighbors (discrete correspondences)
Solving for transformation


Problems:

Nearest neighbor is expensive: O(nm)O(nm) or O(nlogm)O(n \log m)
Correspondences change discontinuously
Cost function is not smooth

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
The NDT Idea
Instead of point-to-point matching:

Convert the target point cloud into a probability density



Divide space into cells (grid)
Fit a Gaussian distribution to points in each cell
Measure how well transformed source points fit the Gaussians



Result: Locally smooth cost function amenable to gradient-based optimization

from matplotlib.patches import Ellipse

def compute_ndt(points, cell_size, x_range, y_range):
    """Compute NDT representation of point cloud."""
    cells = {}

    for i in range(points.shape[1]):
        x, y = points[:, i]
        # Determine cell indices
        cx = int(np.floor((x - x_range[0]) / cell_size))
        cy = int(np.floor((y - y_range[0]) / cell_size))
        key = (cx, cy)

        if key not in cells:
            cells[key] = []
        cells[key].append(points[:, i])

    # Compute statistics for each cell
    ndt = {}
    for key, pts in cells.items():
        pts = np.array(pts).T  # 2 x n
        if pts.shape[1] >= 3:  # Need at least 3 points for covariance
            mean = pts.mean(axis=1)
            centered = pts - mean.reshape(2, 1)
            cov = (centered @ centered.T) / (pts.shape[1] - 1)
            # Add small regularization for numerical stability
            cov += 0.001 * np.eye(2)
            ndt[key] = {'mean': mean, 'cov': cov, 'n_points': pts.shape[1]}

    return ndt

def draw_ellipse(ax, mean, cov, n_std=2, **kwargs):
    """Draw covariance ellipse."""
    eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eigh(cov)
    angle = np.degrees(np.arctan2(eigenvectors[1, 0], eigenvectors[0, 0]))
    width, height = 2 * n_std * np.sqrt(eigenvalues)
    ellipse = Ellipse(mean, width, height, angle=angle, **kwargs)
    ax.add_patch(ellipse)

# Compute NDT
cell_size = 0.5
ndt = compute_ndt(target, cell_size, (-0.5, 4.5), (-0.5, 3.5))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

# Left: Points with grid
axes[0].scatter(target[0], target[1], c='red', s=20, alpha=0.7)
for x in np.arange(-0.5, 4.5, cell_size):
    axes[0].axvline(x, color='gray', alpha=0.3, linewidth=0.5)
for y in np.arange(-0.5, 3.5, cell_size):
    axes[0].axhline(y, color='gray', alpha=0.3, linewidth=0.5)
axes[0].set_title('Target Point Cloud')
axes[0].set_aspect('equal')

# Right: NDT representation
for key, cell in ndt.items():
    draw_ellipse(axes[1], cell['mean'], cell['cov'], n_std=2,
                 fill=True, alpha=0.3, color='blue', edgecolor='blue')
    axes[1].plot(cell['mean'][0], cell['mean'][1], 'b.', markersize=5)

axes[1].set_xlim(-0.7, 4.7); axes[1].set_ylim(-0.7, 3.7)
axes[1].set_title('NDT Representation (2σ ellipses)')
axes[1].set_aspect('equal')

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
The Gaussian Model
Each cell’s Gaussian defines a probability density:
p(x|μk,Σk)=12π|Σk|1/2exp(−12(x−μk)TΣk−1(x−μk))
p(x | \mu_k, \Sigma_k) = \frac{1}{2\pi|\Sigma_k|^{1/2}} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_k)^T \Sigma_k^{-1} (x - \mu_k) \right)


Interpretation: High probability where points are dense, low where sparse


A transformed source point “scores” high if it lands near a cell’s mean

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
The NDT Cost Function
Given transformation parameters T=(θ,tx,ty)T = (\theta, t_x, t_y):
L(T)=−∑i=1nlogp(T(pi))
L(T) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log p\left( T(p_i) \right)


Equivalently (dropping constants): L(T)=∑i=1n(T(pi)−μk(i))TΣk(i)−1(T(pi)−μk(i))
L(T) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T(p_i) - \mu_{k(i)})^T \Sigma_{k(i)}^{-1} (T(p_i) - \mu_{k(i)})

where k(i)k(i) is the cell containing transformed point T(pi)T(p_i)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Cost Function Properties
Smooth within each cell: Quadratic in transformation parameters

Discontinuous at cell boundaries: Cell assignment changes


Solution:

Use larger cells (smoother but less precise)
Or use overlapping grids
Or use soft cell assignment

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
NDT Algorithm: Pseudocode
Input: Source points P, NDT of target (cells with μ, Σ)
Output: Transformation T = (θ, tx, ty)

Initialize T = (0, 0, 0)
repeat:
    # Transform source points
    P' = apply T to P

    # Compute cost and gradient
    L = 0, ∇L = 0
    for each p' in P':
        cell = find_cell(p')
        if cell is occupied:
            L += Mahalanobis_distance(p', μ_cell, Σ_cell)
            ∇L += gradient_contribution(p', μ_cell, Σ_cell)

    # Update transformation (gradient descent or Newton)
    T = T - α * ∇L  (or Newton step)

until convergence
return T

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Computing the Gradient
For a single point pp transformed to p′=R(θ)p+tp' = R(\theta)p + t:
∂L∂θ,∂L∂tx,∂L∂ty
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial t_x}, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial t_y}


Chain rule: Need derivatives of:

Mahalanobis distance w.r.t. p′p'
Transformed point p′p' w.r.t. parameters (θ,tx,ty)(\theta, t_x, t_y)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Gradient Derivation
Let d=p′−μd = p' - \mu and cost term c=dTΣ−1dc = d^T \Sigma^{-1} d

Gradient w.r.t. p′p': ∂c∂p′=2Σ−1d
\frac{\partial c}{\partial p'} = 2 \Sigma^{-1} d



Jacobian of transformation: ∂p′∂(θ,tx,ty)=[−xsinθ−ycosθ10xcosθ−ysinθ01]T
\frac{\partial p'}{\partial (\theta, t_x, t_y)} = \begin{bmatrix} -x\sin\theta - y\cos\theta & 1 & 0 \\ x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
NDT Implementation

def rotation_matrix(theta):
    c, s = np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)
    return np.array([[c, -s], [s, c]])

def transform_points(theta, t, points):
    """Apply transformation to points (2 x N)."""
    R = rotation_matrix(theta)
    return R @ points + t.reshape(2, 1)

def ndt_cost_and_gradient(params, source, ndt, cell_size, x_range, y_range):
    """Compute NDT cost and gradient."""
    theta, tx, ty = params
    t = np.array([tx, ty])

    # Transform source
    transformed = transform_points(theta, t, source)

    cost = 0.0
    grad = np.zeros(3)  # [d/dtheta, d/dtx, d/dty]

    for i in range(source.shape[1]):
        p = source[:, i]
        p_trans = transformed[:, i]

        # Find cell
        cx = int(np.floor((p_trans[0] - x_range[0]) / cell_size))
        cy = int(np.floor((p_trans[1] - y_range[0]) / cell_size))
        key = (cx, cy)

        if key in ndt:
            mu = ndt[key]['mean']
            cov_inv = np.linalg.inv(ndt[key]['cov'])

            d = p_trans - mu
            cost += d @ cov_inv @ d

            # Gradient
            grad_p = 2 * cov_inv @ d

            # Jacobian of transformation
            c, s = np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)
            dp_dtheta = np.array([-p[0]*s - p[1]*c, p[0]*c - p[1]*s])

            grad[0] += grad_p @ dp_dtheta
            grad[1] += grad_p[0]
            grad[2] += grad_p[1]

    return cost, grad

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
NDT Optimization

def ndt_align(source, ndt, cell_size, x_range, y_range,
              max_iters=100, lr=0.01, tol=1e-6):
    """Align source to target using NDT."""
    params = np.array([0.0, 0.0, 0.0])  # theta, tx, ty
    costs = []

    for _ in range(max_iters):
        cost, grad = ndt_cost_and_gradient(
            params, source, ndt, cell_size, x_range, y_range)
        costs.append(cost)

        # Gradient descent with adaptive step size
        step = lr * grad / (np.linalg.norm(grad) + 1e-8)
        params = params - step

        if len(costs) > 1 and abs(costs[-2] - costs[-1]) < tol:
            break

    return params, costs

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
NDT Demo


Code
np.random.seed(42)

# Create source (subset of target, transformed)
source_base = target[:, ::3].copy()  # Subsample
true_theta = np.pi / 8
true_t = np.array([0.3, 0.2])
source = rotation_matrix(-true_theta) @ (source_base - true_t.reshape(2, 1))

# Run NDT alignment
params_est, costs = ndt_align(source, ndt, cell_size, (-0.5, 4.5), (-0.5, 3.5),
                              max_iters=200, lr=0.05)

# Apply estimated transformation
aligned = transform_points(params_est[0], params_est[1:], source)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 4))

# Before

axes[0].set_xlim(-1, 5); axes[0].set_ylim(-1, 4); axes[0].set_aspect('equal')

for key, cell in ndt.items():

axes[1].set_title('After NDT'); axes[1].legend()
axes[1].set_xlim(-1, 5); axes[1].set_ylim(-1, 4); axes[1].set_aspect('equal')

# Convergence

print(f"Est:  θ={np.degrees(params_est[0]):.1f}°, t={params_est[1:].round(3)}")

Cell Size Trade-off

cell_sizes = [0.25, 0.5, 1.0]

for ax, cs in zip(axes, cell_sizes):

Small cells: More accurate, but more discontinuities

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Multi-Resolution NDT
Strategy: Start coarse, refine progressively


Large cells → rough alignment
Medium cells → better alignment
Small cells → fine alignment



Each stage initializes from previous result

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Handling Outliers
Both ICP and NDT are sensitive to outliers

NDT approach: Points in empty cells contribute zero to cost

Acts as implicit outlier rejection
Points without nearby target structure are ignored



Additional strategies:

Threshold Mahalanobis distance
Use robust cost functions (Huber, Cauchy)

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Practical Considerations
Grid alignment matters:

Axis-aligned grids can miss diagonal features
Solution: Use multiple rotated grids or octrees


Numerical stability:

Covariance matrices can be singular (collinear points)
Add small regularization: Σ←Σ+ϵI\Sigma \leftarrow \Sigma + \epsilon I



Boundary effects:

Points near cell boundaries experience discontinuities
Solution: Overlapping grids or interpolation

title: Robot Modeling Control
text:
Summary

NDT representation: Grid of Gaussians captures local structure
Smooth cost function: Enables gradient-based optimization
Fast alignment: O(n)O(n) per iteration vs O(nlogm)O(n \log m) for ICP
Cell size trade-off: Smoothness vs precision
Multi-resolution: Coarse-to-fine for robustness


NDT is particularly useful for real-time applications with large point clouds

Robot Modeling Control
text:
Step 2: Compute Cell Statistics
For each cell kk containing points {p1,p2,…,pn}\{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}:

Mean (centroid): μk=1n∑i=1npi
\mu_k = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i



Covariance matrix: Σk=1n−1∑i=1n(pi−μk)(pi−μk)T
\Sigma_k = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (p_i - \mu_k)(p_i - \mu_k)^T



Each occupied cell now has a 2D Gaussian: 𝒩(μk,Σk)\mathcal{N}(\mu_k, \Sigma_k)

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
Simultaneous Localization and Mapping

SLAM Fundamentals







Instructor: Hasan A. Poonawala  Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering  University of Kentucky, Lexington, KY, USA


Topics:  SLAM Problem Formulation  Kalman Filter for SLAM  Extended Kalman Filter

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
The Localization Problem
A robot is typically unable to measure its position.

Instead, it can measure its position in relation to objects in the world.








The Fundamental Challenge


Can’t use Kalman Filter directly to estimate location when we don’t have a map of objects in the world.





Without a map, we don’t know what to expect as measurement in a state (no sensor model).

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
Motivation for SLAM






Supplementary Material


See additional slides on SLAM motivation and applications (instructor materials)




Key insight: If we don’t have a map, we need to build one while simultaneously figuring out where we are.

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
SLAM: The Core Idea
SLAM tries to simultaneously make sense of:

Where we think we are (localization)
What we think we should be seeing (mapping)


Basic idea: Make the map a part of the state.


We can build measurement and motion models for this expanded state.

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
State Definition
The robot at (xr,yr)(x_r, y_r) measures the location of landmarks li=(lix,liy)l_i = (l_{ix}, l_{iy}) in its frame of reference, which is aligned with the world axis.

State vector: x=[xryrl1xl1yl2xl2y⋮lnlxlnly]
x = \begin{bmatrix} x_r \\ y_r \\ l_{1x} \\ l_{1y} \\ l_{2x} \\ l_{2y} \\ \vdots \\ l_{n_l x} \\ l_{n_l y} \end{bmatrix}

where there are nln_l landmarks.

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
Motion Model
The landmarks are assumed stationary, with robot moving according to: [xryr]t+1=[xryr]t+ut+vt
\begin{bmatrix} x_r \\ y_r \end{bmatrix}_{t+1} = \begin{bmatrix} x_r \\ y_r \end{bmatrix}_{t} + u_t + v_t


System matrices:
AA is (2+2nl)×(2+2nl)(2 + 2n_l) \times (2 + 2n_l), the only non-zero elements being: A1,1=1,A2,2=1A_{1,1} = 1, \quad A_{2,2} = 1


BB is (2+2nl)×2(2 + 2n_l) \times 2, the only non-zero elements being: B1,1=1,B2,2=1B_{1,1} = 1, \quad B_{2,2} = 1

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
Performance Analysis
Performance when robot always sees all landmarks:


The estimated mean position of each landmark is quite close to the actual landmark position, relative to other landmarks




The uncertainty in the landmark position is identical to the uncertainty in initial position, in the long run

The robot can treat the landmark as lying within the 3σ3\sigma ellipse of the landmark mean
To avoid collision with high probability, the 3σ3\sigma ellipse corresponding to its position should lie outside the landmark’s ellipse





The difference between the robot’s true and estimated location depends on sensor accuracy

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
Nonlinear Systems
For linear systems with Gaussian additive noise, the Kalman filter provides the least uncertain estimate.

When the system is nonlinear: xt+1=f(xt,ut)+vtyt=h(xt)+wt
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= f(x_t, u_t) + v_t \\
y_t &= h(x_t) + w_t
\end{aligned}

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
Linearization Approach
Use linearization:
At=∂f∂x|x=μt,Bt=∂f∂u|u=ut,Ct=∂h∂x|x=μtpred
A_t = \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \right|_{x = \mu_t}, \quad
B_t = \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial u} \right|_{u = u_t}, \quad
C_t = \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \right|_{x = \mu_t^{pred}}


A blind application of Kalman filter updates using AtA_t, BtB_t, and CtC_t is not guaranteed to work but often does well in practice.

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
Motion Model
The inputs are the speed ss of the robot along its current heading direction, and the angular velocity ω\omega: ut=[stωt]
u_t = \begin{bmatrix} s_t \\ \omega_t \end{bmatrix}


The motion model is now nonlinear: [xryrθr]t+1=[xryrθr]t+[stcosθrstsinθrωt]+vt=f(xt,ut)
\begin{bmatrix} x_r \\ y_r \\ \theta_r \end{bmatrix}_{t+1} = \begin{bmatrix} x_r \\ y_r \\ \theta_r \end{bmatrix}_{t} + \begin{bmatrix} s_t \cos \theta_r \\ s_t \sin \theta_r \\ \omega_t \end{bmatrix} + v_t = f(x_t, u_t)

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
Linearized Motion Model
We linearize to get part of the AA and BB matrices:
[xryrθr]t+1=[10−sinθrst01cosθrst001][xryrθr]t+[cosθr0sinθr001]ut+vt
\begin{bmatrix} x_r \\ y_r \\ \theta_r \end{bmatrix}_{t+1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -\sin \theta_r \, s_t \\ 0 & 1 & \cos \theta_r \, s_t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_r \\ y_r \\ \theta_r \end{bmatrix}_{t} + \begin{bmatrix} \cos \theta_r & 0 \\ \sin \theta_r & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} u_t + v_t

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
Measurement Model
The position of a landmark lil_i in the robot’s frame becomes:
yi=[cosθr−sinθrsinθrcosθr][lix−xrliy−yr]
y_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos \theta_r & -\sin \theta_r \\ \sin \theta_r & \cos \theta_r \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} l_{ix} - x_r \\ l_{iy} - y_r \end{bmatrix}


The full measurement yy corresponds to stacking the yiy_is.

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
Nonlinear Measurement
The measurement model is nonlinear: y=h(x)≠Cxy = h(x) \neq Cx for any matrix CC.

As mentioned earlier, we linearize: C=∂h∂x|x=μtpred
C = \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \right|_{x = \mu_t^{pred}}

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
Limited Sensing
We’ve assumed that we can see all landmarks at once.

In practice, we can only see nearby landmarks.








Data Association Problem


This limitation creates a data association problem when landmarks look similar to each other. For example, the entry to an office cubicle.

title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
text:
Summary

SLAM Problem: Simultaneously estimate robot location AND map
State Augmentation: Include landmark positions in state vector
Linear Case: Non-rotating robot with aligned frame
EKF Extension: Linearize nonlinear motion/measurement models
Rotating Robot: Full SE(2) motion requires Jacobians
Practical Challenges: Limited sensing, data association
